---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size45[
+ What should government do? "Deciding How to Decide"

+ Introducing the basic economics of pollution
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. ...

<br>

Seminar style, circle the chairs!

This week we explore the policy process, especially as it pertains to environmental problems.


Plan:
1. Talk through the Munger chapter for our high level thinking about policy

2. How does the Munger chapter connect to the economics of pollution?
  + Pollution a public or private concern?
  + Most common failures? 
  + Whose wisdom is relevant? (All three!)

3. Introduce public policy and policy analysis process



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Burke_Quote.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

Let's talk through the Munger chapter.

Munger, M. C. (2000). Deciding How to Decide: "Experts," "The People," and "The Market." In *Analyzing Policy: Choices, Conflicts and Practices* (pp. 30–53). W.W. Norton & Company.

Why does he open with this quote?

Compare and contrast with our definition of politics from last week.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-1.png')
background-size: 60%
background-position: center

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-2.png')
background-size: 75%
background-position: center

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-3.png')
background-size: 55%
background-position: center

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-4.png')
background-size: 67%
background-position: center

???





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size45[
OpenStax. (2016). 12.1 The Economics of Pollution. In *Principles of Economics*.
]

.size45[
+ Market failure
+ Externality / Spillover / Social Costs
  + Negative externality
  + Positive externality
]

???


## Chapter Notes from Glossary

additional external cost
    additional costs incurred by third parties outside the production process when a unit of output is produced

externality
    a market exchange that affects a third party who is outside or “external” to the exchange; sometimes called a “spillover”

market failure
    When the market on its own does not allocate resources efficiently in a way that balances social costs and benefits; externalities are one example of a market failure

negative externality
    a situation where a third party, outside the transaction, suffers from a market transaction by others

positive externality
    a situation where a third party, outside the transaction, benefits from a market transaction by others 

social costs
    costs that include both the private costs incurred by firms and also additional costs incurred by third parties outside the production process, like costs of pollution

spillover
    see externality 



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Fig1_1.png')
background-size: 50%
background-position: right

.pull-left[

<br>

.size30[
Figure 1. Taking Social Costs into Account: A Supply Shift. 

If the firm takes only its own costs of production into account, then its supply curve will be S<sub>private</sub>, and the market equilibrium will occur at E<sub>0</sub>. Accounting for additional external costs of $100 for every unit produced, the firm’s supply curve will be S<sub>social</sub>. The new equilibrium will occur at E<sub>1</sub>.
]]
???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Table2.png')
background-size: 90%
background-position: center

???



    
    
---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-gentle_lines_green.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

# Public policy in the US is...

.size45[
+ a democratic process for solving problems

+ AND, the sum of government activities that have an influence on the lives of citizens

+ AND, an attempt to identify and implement the "best" thing to do given a set of constraints.
]
???

Here are three ways public policy can be defined.

These three perspectives differ a bit based on their key focus but all get mixed up in discussions on public policy.

Always a good idea to be sure which of the three you are talking about when discussing the topic.

<br>

In the first, debates about public policy focus on the process of decision-making.

+ Who should have a say?

<br>

The second is a focus on the "rules" that govern our lives.

<br>

The third is the realm of policy analysis.

Your job as an expert is to help people solve problems by writing "best" policy!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-gentle_lines_green.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

# The Five Stages of Policy Analysis
.size40[
1. Formulate the Problem (Framing)

2. Select Criteria for Making a Decision

3. Evaluate Alternatives Using the Criteria

4. Consider Political and Organizational Constraints

5. Implement and Evaluate
]

???

## Becoming a policy analyst requires:
+ Skills in making an argument to enable us to communicate with the policymaking process.
+ Knowledge of the processes by which US government functions in specific policy areas.
+ Tools for evaluating alternative policy choices.


## Criteria Notes
+ Focus on ends, not means
+ Should imply a measure
+ Should be complete
+ Should be mutually exclusive

## Constraints Notes
+ Does our selected policy have a snowball’s chance of being made law?
+ Political Feasibility: Will elected officials vote for it?
+ Organizational Feasibility: Will appointed officials (bureaucrats) support and implement the law?

## Implement and Evaluate Notes
  + Market Accountability
  + Political Accountability
  + Expert Analysis



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-slate.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle, center, inverse

.size80[**For Thursday**]

.size70[
What are the key elements of a domestic process that aims to solve environmental problems?
]

???

QUESTIONS?
